title: LeNet's Layer Pattern
concept: LeNet's famous structure follows a clear CNN recipe: convolution learns local features, pooling shrinks the map, a deeper convolution learns richer patterns, and a dense head makes the final digit decision.

exercises.prompt: Which pair of building blocks often appears together in a classic CNN body?
exercises.explain: Right — convolution learns local features, and pooling shrinks the feature map.

cards.title: You can now read LeNet like a blueprint
cards.body: After Phase 3, LeNet should not look like a mysterious spell. It is a clear arrangement of blocks you already know: learn patterns, shrink carefully, learn richer patterns, then classify.

cards.title: The classic LeNet flow
cards.body: A simplified LeNet pattern is conv -> pooling -> conv -> pooling -> dense head. The early convolution finds local strokes, pooling compresses the map, the next convolution sees larger combinations, and the dense layers turn the learned features into a final digit label.

cards.title: Scout, summarize, decide
cards.body: Imagine a kitchen team. One person inspects the ingredients closely, another organizes them into a shorter summary, then a more experienced cook sees bigger patterns, and finally the head chef chooses the final dish. LeNet stages information in a similar way.

cards.title: From pixel grid to digit class
cards.body: A small digit image enters the first convolution stage, which reacts to edges and tiny curves. Pooling trims the spatial size. A second convolution combines those pieces into more complete shapes like loops or angled strokes. After another shrinking step, the dense head decides whether the image is a 3, 8, or another class.

cards.title: The order is not random
cards.body: LeNet's layers are not a shuffled pile. Pooling after feature extraction helps save compute and widen context, while deeper convolution after that can work on richer, more compact feature maps. The arrangement is part of the design logic.

cards.title: Phase 3 blocks inside LeNet
cards.body: The famous architecture is mostly a concrete recipe built from familiar CNN pieces.

left.text:
Convolution learns local patterns
Pooling shrinks maps
Dense head chooses class

right.text:
Conv -> Pool -> Conv -> Pool
Then a classifier head
Same blocks, named design

exercises.prompt: Which sequence best matches LeNet's classic layer pattern?
exercises.explain: Exactly — that is the standard beginner summary of LeNet's body and head.

options.text: Conv -> Pool -> Conv -> Pool -> Dense head

exercises.prompt: True or false: In LeNet, pooling helps shrink feature maps between convolution stages.
exercises.explain: True — that shrinking step is part of the classic LeNet pattern.
Pooling is right — that block is the classic shrinking step between convolution stages.
exercises.sentence: In LeNet, ___ reduces spatial size so later layers work on a smaller summary map.

exercises.prompt: Why does a second convolution stage help after the first shrinking step?
exercises.explain: Right — once early edges and curves are summarized, the deeper conv can combine them into richer shapes.

options.text: It can combine simpler earlier features into larger digit parts on a more compact map
It turns the network into a text editor instead of an image model
It removes the need for any final classifier head

exercises.prompt: A learner says, 'I finished Phase 3, so what exactly am I seeing inside LeNet?' Which answer is best?
exercises.explain: Exactly. LeNet is a famous example of the same building blocks you already know: convolutions to learn features, pooling to shrink maps, then a dense classifier head to choose the digit.

options.text: A named CNN recipe built from familiar blocks: convolution stages, shrinking steps, and a final dense classifier head
A model that skips feature extraction and reads digits using only one giant dense layer on raw pixels
A system where pooling replaces the need for convolutions entirely
A network designed mainly to store image filenames instead of visual patterns
